title: Imperial Letter
html:
Father is already at the table when I come down.

He is reading a letter, which he sets face-down the moment he sees me, with the unhurried care of someone who wants the gesture to look like it was going to happen anyway. It was not. I note the paper's seal color, dark blue and official-looking, before it disappears under his hand, and I sit down across from him and reach for the bread without mentioning it.

"Good morning," he says.

"Good morning, Father."

He studies me for a moment with the expression he has been wearing since last night, the one that is trying very hard to look like his normal expression. He is not quite managing it. There is something in the set of his jaw that was not there yesterday, a kind of alertness that keeps surfacing and being put back. He has been awake for a while, I think.

"Did you sleep well?"

"I did." I cut the bread and spread it with the dark honey that the cook keeps on the breakfast table, which is one of the genuinely good things about this household. "I wanted to ask you something."

"Of course."

"I'd like to hire a sword instructor. Someone who can start immediately and is willing to work around a schedule I'm still building." I keep my voice even and practical. "I don't have prior training, so I need someone who can begin from the foundation. Do you have a recommendation, or should I make inquiries through another channel?"

The expression on his face goes through several things in a short period of time. He lands on careful.

"Sword training," he repeats.

"Yes."

"Elena." He sets down his cup. "I think that's... yes, I think that's wise. I will make inquiries today." A pause. "Sword training."

"Thank you, Father."

He looks at his cup for a moment. Then he looks at the letter under his hand, just a glance, quickly redirected. "There are one or two names I can ask about. Men who train the younger sons of noble families. I'll send letters this afternoon."

"One who won't ask too many questions about why I'm starting at 16 would be preferable."

"I'll see what I can do." He picks up his cup again, then sets it down again without drinking from it. "Is there anything else you need?"

"Nothing right now. Thank you."

He nods. He is doing the thing where he wants to say something and has decided not to, which I have seen him do twice before, both times about the title. I let him not say it.

Mother arrives four minutes later, which is late enough that she and Father had clearly discussed the morning's timing. She greets me with a warmth that is about ten degrees warmer than her usual warmth, sits down, and begins arranging things on the table that do not need arranging.

"Elena, dear," she says, in the voice of someone who has rehearsed a casual opening, "I thought we might sit together this morning, if you have time."

"I have time."

"I thought we might discuss the coming-of-age ceremony." She looks at me with the expression of someone offering a gift they are not entirely sure will be received. "It's next month, as you know. I wasn't sure whether you were planning to attend, given..." She stops. "But if you'd like to go, I would love to help you prepare."

There it is. Given the family's position, and the court's opinion of the family's position, and Elena's previous pattern of making herself invisible, Mother had apparently already written off the ceremony as something they were going to quietly not mention until it passed. The fact that she is bringing it up at all means last night shifted something for her.

"I'm planning to attend," I say. "And I was hoping you'd be willing to teach me what I need to know. Etiquette, precedence, the families I'll be expected to recognize."

Both of her hands come up to her face. "Oh, Elena."

I wait.

"Of course," she says, collecting herself with visible effort. "Of course I will. We have time, if we start today." She straightens in her chair, and the professional quality that I have occasionally glimpsed underneath her usual softness surfaces fully. "There is quite a lot to cover. The seating precedence alone will take two sessions, and the formal waltz requires a partner to practice properly, which means we'll need to arrange something. The fashion I can advise on but the actual commission needs to go to the dressmaker by the end of this week at the latest, or we won't have it in time."

"I was planning to handle the fashion myself."

She looks at me. "You have opinions about fashion?"

"I have opinions about what works on this face. Yes."

A pause, then something that might be the beginning of a smile. "Then we'll go together. I can handle the introduction to Madam Severin and you can tell her what you want." She folds her hands on the table. "Etiquette this afternoon? I can clear my schedule."

"This afternoon works."

Father is watching us with an expression I have not seen on him before. I cannot fully classify it, except that it is not unhappy.

***

The etiquette session runs until the light through the sitting room windows goes amber.

Mother works from a mental list she has apparently been maintaining for years, possibly since Elena was born. Court forms of address, the hierarchy of greetings by rank, the specific sequence of gestures required when being introduced to members of the imperial family versus high nobility versus ordinary nobility, the difference between a dismissive nod and an acknowledging one and why getting them mixed up is a problem. She covers each item with the focused precision of someone who cares about this professionally, which is different from the way she moves through the dining room or the garden. Here she is not soft. She is exact.

I take notes in the empire's script, which I can now write quickly enough that I do not fall behind. The forms of address alone cover four pages. The precedence chart for greetings requires a diagram.

"When you enter the ceremony hall," she says, "you will be announced. The announcement covers title and family name only. Nothing else." She pauses. "Given our current standing, the announcement will not draw much attention. That is, it has the advantage of giving you time to observe the room before anyone focuses on you."

It is the most direct she has been about the family's position, and she delivers it without flinching. I find I respect that.

"That's actually useful," I say.

She looks at me. "You think so?"

"If no one is watching when I walk in, I can watch them instead. That's better."

The expression on her face does the thing where something that has been held carefully for a long time gets set down. Just for a moment. She picks it back up, but I saw it.

"The waltz," she says, returning to her notes. "I'll arrange for you to practice with someone before the ceremony. One of the young men from a neighboring house, perhaps. Someone who won't gossip."

"I can manage the basic steps on my own first. Just to not waste their time."

"There is a music room on the second floor. The floor is good." She stands and begins gathering her notes. "I'll have the sheet music sent up. The standard court waltz uses three variations of the base pattern. You'll need all three."

I nod and close my own notes.

"Elena." She says my name the way she did at the beginning of the meal last night, the way that means she is not sure whether to continue. She continues. "I know you've been..." She stops. "I know things have been difficult. For a while." She is looking at the notes in her hands rather than at me. "I wasn't sure how to help. I should have tried harder."

I look at her for a moment.

The woman in front of me tried to set a careful table last night because her daughter was eating dinner with her. She spent three hours this afternoon covering court etiquette with the focus of someone who has been saving this particular set of skills for exactly this use. Whatever the previous Elena went through, it was not because her mother did not care.

"You're helping now," I say.

She looks up. Her jaw is doing the thing Father's does when he is deciding not to say something. She decides not to say it, and nods, and takes her notes out of the room.

I sit in the sitting room for a moment after she leaves. The amber light has gone to grey. Outside, I can hear the household staff beginning the evening routines, the sound of water being carried up and doors being checked.

Five months.

The list as of tonight: sword instructor pending Father's inquiries, etiquette in progress, waltz practice to arrange, dressmaker appointment tomorrow with Mother, magic solo practice ongoing. The margin note is in the unverified column and will stay there until I find another data point. The ceremony is in 29 days.

I get up to go upstairs and nearly walk into Lily, who has been waiting outside the sitting room door with the particular stillness of someone who has been there for some time.

"My lady," she says. "There is a letter. It arrived an hour ago, while you were with your mother."

She holds it out. The seal is pressed in dark blue wax, and the crest stamped into it is one I recognize from the novel's background chapters, a pair of crossed lances over a crown.

The imperial household.

I take the letter and turn it over. My name is on the front in a secretary's formal hand. Not Father's name. Mine.

'Well,' I think. 'That's inconvenient.'
